---
title: "Setup and organisation"
author: "Gary Napier gary.napier@ccn.ac.uk"
format:
  revealjs:
    smaller: true
    logo: img/ccn_logo.png
    css: setup_and_organisation.css
    footer: "Access to Computing - setup and organisation"
    embed-resources: true
    output-file: index.html
---

## Learning outcomes / overview

- Why organisation is important, especially in computing
- Projects as the "unit" of organisation
- Directory structure
- File versions
- Working on different machines
- Reproducibility

# Why organisation is important

## Why organisation is important

- "Organisation is key to productivity"
- You don't want to get into a mess with trying to find files
- If you do find the "right" file, which version is correct?
- Do everything for "future you"

![](img/confused.jpg)

# Projects as the "unit" of organisation

## Projects as the "unit" of organisation

- You need a "one folder, one project" mental model
- The folder should be a coherent body of work where everything related to that *project* sits
- Took me ages to realise this!

## Projects as the "unit" of organisation

- DON'T have folders that are for e.g. 
  - Any and all types of scripts or files, e.g. a folder called `python_scripts` вќЊ
    - But you **could** have a folder called `python_scripts` that is a **subfolder** within a **project** - i.e. the Python scripts are *only* relevant to that project

  - Work that's from a particular location or context (see my Github repo mistakes later)  вќЊ
  
## Projects as the "unit" of organisation

- See Chapter 2 of [Bioinformatics Data Skills](https://womengovtcollegevisakha.ac.in/departments/Bioinformatics%20Data%20Skills%20Reproducible%20and%20Robust%20Research%20with%20Open%20Source%20Tools%20by%20Vince%20Buffalo.pdf){target="_blank"}

> "Creating a well-organized directory structure is the foundation of a reproducible bioinformatics project" - Vince Buffalo (p.21)

- The "one folder per project" model is not just for your own organisation
- It helps with programming as it allows you to...
  - automate data processing/computational tasks much easier
  - package up your project and its environment to make into software or make reproducible

## Projects as the "unit" of organisation

- So what should your project directories look like?
- Something like this..

```
$ tree project_example/
project_example/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ cache
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ config
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ global.dcf
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ <data files>
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ clean_and_wrangle
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ <scripts>
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ plots
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ <scripts>
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в””в”Ђв”Ђ analysis
    в”њв”Ђв”Ђ <scripts>
    в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
```

## Projects as the "unit" of organisation

- Each of these might have a subfolder, e.g. you might want to divide your `data` into `csv`, `fasta`, whatever's appropriate and keeps you organised. 

```
$ tree project_example/
project_example/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ cache
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ config
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ global.dcf
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ <data files>
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ clean_and_wrangle
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ <scripts>
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ plots
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ <scripts>
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в””в”Ђв”Ђ analysis
    в”њв”Ђв”Ђ <scripts>
    в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
```

## Projects as the "unit" of organisation

- Notice each folder has a README file. 
- It's also super important to have good documentation in your projects
  - For others
  - **For "future you"**

```
$ tree project_example/
project_example/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ cache
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ config
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ global.dcf
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ <data files>
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ clean_and_wrangle
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ <scripts>
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ plots
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ <scripts>
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
в””в”Ђв”Ђ analysis
    в”њв”Ђв”Ђ <scripts>
    в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md
```

## Projects as the "unit" of organisation

- See also the R package site [Project Template](http://projecttemplate.net/){target="_blank"}
- Good example of software that automates project structure
- Good "philosophy" of organisation
- See the [Architecture](http://projecttemplate.net/architecture.html){target="_blank"} section for layouts and types of files commonly used in an analysis project

## Projects as the "unit" of organisation

[My Github repos](https://github.com/GaryNapier?tab=repositories){target="_blank"}

- Let's look at some mistakes here!

## Projects as the "unit" of organisation

- Excellent example of project structure - [Tiny Python Projects](https://github.com/kyclark/tiny_python_projects/tree/master){target="_blank"}
- Subfolders for each discrete task/sub-project
- Named clearly and numbered sequentially

# Versions

## File versions

- Never, ever, EVER doing this.. Your `project` directory looks like this:
```
project_rough.doc
project_final.doc
project_final_edit.doc
project_final_edit_1.doc
project_FINAL.doc
project_FINAL_1.doc
project_FINAL_FINAL.doc
project_FINAL_FINAL_HAND_IN.doc
project_FINAL_FINAL_HAND_IN_THIS_REALLY_IS_MY_DEFINITIVE_FINAL_VERSION.doc
project_2.doc
```

- I've done this so you don't have to. 

## File versions

- ONE file, ONE version
  - Ok, you might have a separate file that is experimental with rough workings, but make this VERY clear in its name. 
  - NO MORE THAN ONE rough/experimental version... PLEASE!
  
- How can I work on this ONE file if I'm at home one day and at CC the next... And back again?
  - Next section - *Working on different machines*

# Working on different machines

## Working on different machines - problem

- You start work on a file at City College.. e.g. `programming_task.py`
- That evening you need to work on THE SAME file (`programming_task.py`) 
  - (NOT a different version of the work in a different file e.g. `programming_task_HOME_VERSION.py`)
- How do you then access the same file (`programming_task.py`) at home?

## Working on different machines - solution

- email it to yourself? - NO вќЊ
- memory stick? - NO вќЊ

- Cloud - YES вњ…
- Github (or Bitbucket, etc) - YES вњ…

::: {layout-ncol=2}
![](img/cloud.png){width=300}

![](img/github_logo.png){width=300}
:::


## Working on different machines - solution

- Sub-lecture - [Setup lecture after making LOTS of mistakes](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1IXz_5pn1aqkKsRSgdeXwmgHAVR5R6GR5Xe58IcrOJvw/edit?usp=sharing){target="_blank"}

# Reproducibility

## Reproducibility

- This is most important for science, data analysis and things like that where you have pipelines
- But still relevant to software development, or anything that packages things up into discrete units 

## Reproducibility

- You should be able to have a "master script" that runs all your other scripts and just works, first time on any machine
- Your scripts should be able to take different data but with the same format (more rows for example), but process it in the same way
- Same analysis, different results

## Reproducibility

- This means you have to think of ways when programming to represent variables or concepts flexibly
- i.e. NO HARDCODING!

## Reproducibility

- NO HARDCODING!
- Simple example - you need to calculate three months ago from today, every time the script is run. 

- Pseudo code:

  - NO  вќЊ
  ```
  three_mo_ago = "2026-04-24" - 3
  ```
  
  - Yes вњ…
  ```
  three_mo_ago = date_today() - 3
  ```
  
  - Yes, better вњ…
  ```
  todays_date = date_today()
  months_ago_from_today = 3
  three_mo_ago = todays_date - months_ago_from_today
  ```

# General tips

## File names

> "[Spaces aren't] appropriate in a Unix-based environment, because spaces are used to separate arguments in [Unix] commands." - Vince Buffalo (p.23)

## File names

- DO NOT have spaces in your file names - this makes it really hard to process the file names as data 
  - You have to wrap the filename in backticks ("```") and is a pain for you and for programming when you want to call file names. 
- Use underscores or dashes
  
  - Bad вќЊ - `my project/python scripts/data cleaning and wrangling.py`
  - Good вњ… - `my_project/python_scripts/data_cleaning_and_wrangling.py`
  - Good вњ… - `my-project/python-scripts/data-cleaning-and-wrangling.py`
  
- Get in the habit of this, naming every file you ever create as something informative and without spaces, even if the file seems unimportant

## File names

- Number ordered - especially if the scripts run in sequence
  - Good вњ… `my_project/python_scripts/01_data_cleaning_and_wrangling.py`
- Dates
  - Good вњ… - `my_project/python_scripts/01_data_cleaning_and_wrangling_20260403.py`
- short but descriptive
  - I'd rather use very long names and know what the file is than a short name and not know


## Further reading and resources

[Bioinformatics Data Skills](https://womengovtcollegevisakha.ac.in/departments/Bioinformatics%20Data%20Skills%20Reproducible%20and%20Robust%20Research%20with%20Open%20Source%20Tools%20by%20Vince%20Buffalo.pdf){target="_blank"}

[Happy Git with R](https://happygitwithr.com/)
